unkeyed_headers.description: Web cache poisoning via unkeyed headers — inject values into cached responses by abusing headers the cache ignores when computing the cache key

probes.description: X-Forwarded-Host injection — if swktest-<marker>.evil.example appears in a script src, link href, or redirect in response body AND cache headers present, the header is unkeyed and poisons cached content
X-Forwarded-Scheme downgrade — forces 301/302 redirect to HTTP, poisoning cache with redirect loop or protocol downgrade
X-Original-URL override — backend processes /admin instead of requested path; response length diff indicates path override worked

cache_deception.description: Web cache deception — trick the cache into storing authenticated responses by appending static file extensions to dynamic URLs

probes.description: Path confusion with .css extension — cache stores authenticated page content as static CSS resource, leaking sensitive data to unauthenticated users
Semicolon path parameter with .js extension — backend ignores semicolon suffix, cache keys on full path including .js and stores authenticated content
URL-encoded slash with .css extension — path normalization discrepancy between cache and backend causes authenticated content to be cached as static file

cloudflare_cache_bypass.description: Cloudflare-specific cache key normalization quirks. Cloudflare treats some query-string variants as identical cache keys, allowing variant-based poisoning. Detection: response carries CF-Ray, CF-Cache-Status, or Server: cloudflare.

probes.description: Trailing-empty-param trick — ?x=1 and ?x=1& may share cache key. Test by polluting one variant and checking the other.
Fragment in query — Cloudflare strips fragments before keying but origin may receive full URL. Differential pollution possible.
X-HTTP-Method-Override on cached path — Cloudflare keys on method but origin honors override. Cache HIT for the override response = poisoning.

fastly_normalization.description: Fastly-specific cache normalization. Fastly canonicalizes %2f / / and some Unicode variants before keying, leading to differential responses between cache and origin.

probes.description: %2f normalization — Fastly may collapse %2f to / for keying while origin treats them differently.
Path traversal normalized — Fastly may resolve ../ before keying. Origin path differs from cache key.

akamai_param_order.description: Akamai Edge — query-string parameter order may not be normalized in cache key. ?a=1&b=2 and ?b=2&a=1 can be distinct keys, allowing per-order poisoning.

probes.description: Reverse query-parameter order — if Akamai keys on raw query string and origin accepts both orders, two distinct cached versions exist. Poison the less-common one and survive cache invalidation longer.
Akamai Pragma debug — some Akamai configs leak cache key and origin URL via X-Cache-Key / X-True-Cache-Key headers. Useful intel for crafting collisions.

head_request_caching.description: HEAD-request caching — some caches store HEAD responses keyed by URL only, then serve as GET responses. Browsers don't typically issue HEAD but caches may serve HEAD-cached body to GET clients.

probes.description: HEAD then GET — issue HEAD to populate cache, then GET. Compare bodies. If GET serves a HEAD-style empty body or different metadata, cache layer is conflating methods.

vary_header_bypass.description: Vary header bypass — cache ignores Accept-Encoding or other Vary headers, serving wrong-encoding responses to clients that can't decode them. Combined with Content-Type confusion, can deliver attacker payload as text/html instead of application/json.

probes.description: Send GET with Accept-Encoding: identity and a poison value, then GET with Accept-Encoding: gzip. If both responses match (cache ignored Vary), encoding confusion is possible.

parameter_cloaking.description: Cache key manipulation via parameter cloaking — exploit cache parameter parsing discrepancies to inject unkeyed parameters into cached responses

probes.description: Semicolon parameter cloaking — cache excludes utm_content from key but backend parses semicolon as parameter delimiter, reflecting the evil parameter value in cached response
Fat GET request — send GET with body parameters; cache ignores request body when computing key but backend processes body params, poisoning cached response with attacker-controlled values
